candidate_questions.question: How do you expect to search for stocks? For example, by ticker or company name, or do you need to filter by criteria like sector, market cap, or other attributes?
candidate_questions.rationale: This distinguishes simple search (H1) from advanced filtering (H2, H3, H4).

possible_answers.answer: By multiple criteria including sector, market cap, etc.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on simple search by ticker/name.
H2 emphasizes advanced criteria filtering.
H3 may need both, but real-time dashboard often supports advanced search.
Portfolio tracking likely needs simple ticker entry plus some filtering.

candidate_questions.question: What kind of analysis reports should be generated? For example, a quick summary with key metrics, or a detailed report with charts and narrative, or both?
candidate_questions.rationale: This distinguishes report detail level: summary (H1) vs. detailed (H2) vs. customizable (H3, H4).

possible_answers.answer: Only a quick summary (e.g., price, news, ratios)
A detailed report (e.g., full financials, charts, analysis text)

likelihoods.rationale: H1 assumes summary report.
H2 is about detailed PDF reports.
H3 likely offers both due to customizability.
Portfolio reports may need summary for overview, detailed for analysis.

candidate_questions.question: Should the report generation be automatic every time you search, or should you be able to trigger it manually or configure when it runs?
candidate_questions.rationale: This distinguishes automation model: always automatic (H1, H4 periodic) vs. configurable (H3) vs. manual (H2).

likelihoods.rationale: H1 says auto-generate.
H2 detailed PDF may be generated on request.
H3 real-time dashboard likely offers configurable generation.
H4 portfolio tracking suggests periodic automatic reports, maybe with manual on-demand.

candidate_questions.question: Do you need to save and track reports for multiple stocks over time, such as a portfolio view with historical comparisons?
candidate_questions.rationale: This distinguishes single-report systems (H1, H2, H3) from portfolio tracking (H4).

possible_answers.answer: No, just search and get a report each time
Yes, I want to save stocks and track them with periodic reports

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is focused on single searches.
H2 may generate reports but not necessarily track.
H3 dashboard could include watchlist, but not primary.
H4 is all about tracking and periodic reports.

candidate_questions.question: When you generate a report, would you prefer it to be an interactive dashboard with charts that you can customize, or a static document like a PDF or a one-page summary?
candidate_questions.rationale: This question directly addresses the report delivery dimension, distinguishing between static documents (H1, H2) and interactive dashboards (H3). H4 could go either way.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is a summary report, likely static.
H2 explicitly mentions PDF, so strongly static.
H3 is a real-time dashboard, so interactive.
H4 could involve either static periodic reports or interactive dashboard tracking.

candidate_questions.question: Should the report be generated automatically as soon as you search for a stock, or would you prefer to click a button to generate it on demand?
candidate_questions.rationale: This distinguishes automation model: always auto (H1) vs on-demand (H3) vs periodic (H4). H2 could be either.

possible_answers.answer: On demand by clicking a generate button

likelihoods.rationale: H1 says auto-generate.
H2 does not specify automation, could be either.
H3 says on-demand generation.
H4 periodic automated reports, but generation might be scheduled, not triggered by search.

candidate_questions.rationale: This probes the persistence/tracking dimension, distinguishing single-stock (H1, H2, H3) from portfolio monitoring (H4).

possible_answers.answer: I want to search and analyze one stock at a time
I want to maintain a list of stocks I follow and get reports on them

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is single stock search.
H2 is per-stock report, but could support list.
H3 dashboard could have a watchlist.
H4 explicitly portfolio tracking.

candidate_questions.question: Who is the primary target audience for the poker platform?
candidate_questions.rationale: Different hypotheses target different user groups: casual players, serious gamblers, beginners, or social gamers. This question should help distinguish them.

possible_answers.answer: Casual players looking for fun and social interaction
Competitive players seeking real money and rankings
Beginners wanting to learn the game

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is casual, free-to-play.
H2 is for competitive, real-money players.
H3 is educational, targeting beginners.
H4 covers both casual and competitive.

candidate_questions.question: What type of opponents do you envision?
candidate_questions.rationale: This helps differentiate between AI-only, human opponents, or both, which is a key feature distinction.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is multiplayer with humans.
H2 requires real human opponents for real money.
H3 is educational with AI.
H4 may include both, but primarily human.

candidate_questions.question: How important is security and fair play assurance?
candidate_questions.rationale: Security level is a major disagreement. Real-money platforms need high security, while free-to-play can have low security.

possible_answers.answer: Very important, must have anti-cheat and secure transactions
Moderately important, basic security is enough
Not important, security is not a concern

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is free-to-play, low security.
H2 requires high security for real money.
H3 educational, moderate security.
H4 likely needs high security if real money.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 includes basic chat.
H2 may have competitive social features.
H3 educational, minimal social.
H4 emphasizes community and social.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: Basic credit repair website with informational service pages, a self-assessment credit score tool (user inputs data manually), and a contact form that emails leads. No user accounts or CRM integration.
Advanced credit repair website with third-party credit score API integration, user registration to save progress and retrieve scores, and automated lead capture into a CRM for follow-up.
Educational credit repair blog/resource site with a free soft-pull credit score check, an appointment booking system for consultations, and email-based lead notifications.
Compliance-heavy credit repair site with legal disclaimers, a simple lead form that triggers a phone call, and emphasis on trust signals. Credit score check is optional or not included.

User account requirement (none vs optional vs mandatory)

A full-featured real estate website with user accounts, saved searches, property alerts, and direct agent messaging. Includes both residential and commercial listings with advanced filters and map integration.

user features (no accounts vs accounts with saved searches and alerts)

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A personal email client for individuals focused on basic compose/send and simple contact management, possibly with a clean interface and minimal extra features.
A business email platform with advanced features like templates, scheduling, multi-account support, and integration with calendars and CRM systems.

turns.user: I do not have a strong preference about that, or it is not important to me.

judge.response: I do not have a strong preference about that, or it is not important to me.

evaluator.reasoning: The question asks broadly about important features for the recruitment process, which does not directly target any specific hidden implicit requirement. None of the requirements are directly elicited by this question.
evaluator.response: I do not have a strong preference about that, or it is not important to me.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A simple job board where drivers can browse and apply to job listings posted by employers, with basic search and filter capabilities.
A platform with driver verification (license, background checks) and employer ratings, plus matching based on location, vehicle type, and availability.
A system focusing on real-time job alerts and mobile access, allowing drivers to receive push notifications for new jobs matching their profile.
A managed marketplace where the platform facilitates contracts, payments, and dispute resolution between drivers and employers.

hypothesis_disagreements: Core functionality scope (simple listings vs. full-service marketplace)
update_notes: Initial broad requirement; four diverse hypotheses represent different interpretations.

candidate_questions.question: What are the most important features you envision for the recruitment process?
candidate_questions.rationale: To distinguish between a simple job board and a more feature-rich platform with verification and matching.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is a simple job board.
H2 emphasizes verification and matching.
H3 includes alerts but may still have matching.
H4 is a marketplace with advanced features.

candidate_questions.question: Should the platform handle any aspects of the hiring workflow beyond posting and applying, such as scheduling interviews or onboarding?
candidate_questions.rationale: To determine the depth of process support.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 minimal.
H2 may include communication.
H3 focuses on alerts, but scheduling possible.
H4 includes full lifecycle.

candidate_questions.question: What types of drivers and jobs are you targeting? For example, long-haul trucking, local delivery, rideshare, or all?
candidate_questions.rationale: To clarify the scope of the driver/job categories, which affects system design.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 generic.
H2 could be broad or focused.
H3 alerts for all.
H4 marketplace likely for specific segments.

candidate_questions.question: How important is it for employers to have analytics or reporting on their job postings and applicant pools?
candidate_questions.rationale: To distinguish between basic posting and employer management tools.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 no analytics.
H2 may have basic stats.
H3 alerts may not include analytics.
H4 marketplace needs analytics.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A simple job board where drivers can browse and apply to job listings posted by employers, with basic search and filter capabilities.
A platform with driver verification (license, background checks) and employer ratings, plus matching based on location, vehicle type, and availability.
A system focusing on real-time job alerts and mobile access, allowing drivers to receive push notifications for new jobs matching their profile.
A managed marketplace where the platform facilitates contracts, payments, and dispute resolution between drivers and employers.

candidate_questions.question: How should the platform generate revenue?
candidate_questions.rationale: The business model is a key differentiator: H1 and H3 typically rely on employer-paid postings, H2 may use a mix, and H4 often involves commission per hire.

likelihoods.rationale: Simple job boards often charge employers per posting.
Verification and matching could be monetized via both posting fees and commissions.
Real-time alerts might be offered free with premium postings.
Managed marketplace typically takes a commission on transactions.

candidate_questions.question: How important is it to verify driver credentials (license, background) before allowing them to apply?
candidate_questions.rationale: This directly tests the trust and safety dimension: H2 and H4 emphasize verification, while H1 and H3 may not require it.

likelihoods.rationale: Simple boards rarely require verification.
Verification is a core feature.
Alerts can work without verification, but may be nice to have.
Trust is essential in a managed marketplace.

candidate_questions.question: How important are real-time notifications for drivers when new jobs are posted?
candidate_questions.rationale: This directly tests the emphasis on real-time features: H3 prioritizes notifications, while others may not.

likelihoods.rationale: Static listings do not require real-time updates.
Matching can benefit from notifications but not essential.
Real-time alerts are the core differentiator.
Marketplace may have notifications but not the primary focus.

candidate_questions.question: What kind of tools should employers have to manage the recruitment process?
candidate_questions.rationale: This explores the employer-side depth: H1 offers basic posting, H2 may include tracking, H4 includes contract/payment management.

likelihoods.rationale: Simple board only needs posting.
Ratings and matching imply some tracking.
Alerts focus on drivers; employer tools minimal.
Managed marketplace requires contract and payment tools.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A platform for readers and researchers to access medical articles, with personal account features for saving, preferences, and subscription management.
A full journal management system allowing authors to submit articles, track reviews, and manage submissions, in addition to reading.
A system for reviewers and editors to manage peer review, assign reviewers, make decisions, and communicate, alongside reading access.
A hybrid platform for a medical journal society where members can read, contribute content, participate in discussions, and manage their membership.

nature of involvement (personal reading management vs submissions/reviews vs membership)
lifecycle scope (dissemination only vs full submission-to-publication)
collaboration features (none vs limited vs extensive)

latent_complete_requirement_hypotheses.complete_requirement: A platform for readers and researchers to access medical articles, with personal account features for saving, preferences, and subscription management.
A full journal management system allowing authors to submit articles, track reviews, and manage submissions, in addition to reading.

candidate_questions.question: What types of users do you envision interacting with this system?
candidate_questions.rationale: Identifying primary user roles (readers, authors, reviewers, editors, society members) will help focus the system design.

possible_answers.answer: All roles including editors and society members

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on readers, so readers-only is most likely.
H2 emphasizes authors and reviewers, so that answer type is most probable.
H3 is similar to H2 but for reviewers/editors, still authors/reviewers is most likely.
H4 includes all roles for a society, so all roles is most probable.

candidate_questions.question: What kind of involvement with the journal do you want users to manage?
candidate_questions.rationale: Understanding the nature of involvement (reading management, submissions/reviews, membership) will clarify core functionality.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 emphasizes personal reading management.
H2 focuses on submissions and reviews.
H3 also centers on submissions/reviews (review management).
H4 highlights membership and subscriptions.

candidate_questions.question: Should the system support the entire article lifecycle from submission to publication, or focus mainly on dissemination?
candidate_questions.rationale: This distinguishes between a full publishing platform and a repository or reading site.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is about reading, so dissemination focus.
H2 requires full lifecycle for submissions.
H3 needs full lifecycle for review management.
H4 likely involves both reading and contributions.

candidate_questions.question: Are there specific features for collaboration or discussion among users?
candidate_questions.rationale: Collaboration features (comments, forums, peer review discussions) help differentiate between simple reading and community-oriented platforms.

possible_answers.answer: Extensive collaboration (discussions, forums, peer review communication)

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on individual reading, so minimal collaboration.
H2 may have some collaboration via reviews, but not extensive.
H3 involves extensive peer review communication.
H4 likely includes discussions and community features.

latent_complete_requirement_hypotheses.complete_requirement: A platform for readers and researchers to access medical articles, with personal account features for saving, preferences, and subscription management.

candidate_questions.question: Which users do you envision using this platform?
candidate_questions.rationale: To distinguish between platforms for readers only, authors, reviewers, or all roles.

possible_answers.answer: All including editors and society members

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on readers, so likely 'only readers'.
H2 includes authors, so 'authors and reviewers' likely.
H3 includes reviewers/editors, so might lean to 'all'.
H4 is society-oriented, so 'all including society members'.

candidate_questions.question: What kind of content management do you need? For example, do you need to submit articles or just read?
candidate_questions.rationale: To distinguish H1 (reading only) from H2/H3 (submission and review).

likelihoods.rationale: H1 lacks submission features.
H2 includes submission.
H3 may include submission for reviewers, but not necessarily.
H4 includes content contribution, so submission likely.

candidate_questions.question: Is there a need for peer review functionality?
candidate_questions.rationale: To specifically identify H3 (reviewer/editor system).

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is reader-focused, no review.
H2 for authors may include some review but not central.
H3 centers on review.
H4 may or may not include peer review.

candidate_questions.question: Do you need membership management features like subscriptions or society administration?
candidate_questions.rationale: To identify H4 (society/membership focus).

likelihoods.rationale: H1 may have subscriptions but not full admin.
H2 could include institution subscriptions.
H3 might need editor assignment but not membership.
H4 explicitly involves society membership.

candidate_questions.question: Could you describe what you mean by managing your involvement with the journal?
candidate_questions.rationale: This directly targets the core ambiguity about the user's intended activities beyond reading.

possible_answers.answer: Personal reading management (e.g., save articles, set preferences, subscriptions)
Article submission and tracking (e.g., submit manuscripts, see reviews)
Peer review management (e.g., handle review assignments, write reviews)
Membership management (e.g., pay dues, access member-only content)

likelihoods.rationale: H1 assumes personal reading management.
H2 emphasizes author submission features.
H3 centers on peer review management.
H4 includes membership management as core.

candidate_questions.question: What is your primary role in relation to medical journals?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifying the user's role helps determine which features are essential.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 assumes reader role.
H2 assumes author role.
H3 assumes reviewer/editor role.
H4 allows various roles, but member identity is key.

candidate_questions.question: Do you need the system to support multiple roles (e.g., both reading and submitting), or is a single role sufficient?
candidate_questions.rationale: This determines whether the system must handle multiple user types and workflows.

possible_answers.answer: Multiple roles should be supported for the same user
The system should support different roles for different users

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on reader role but may accept multiple.
H2 involves authors who may also read.
H3 involves reviewers who are often also authors.
H4 typically has multiple distinct roles (members, editors).

candidate_questions.question: What kind of content are you most interested in? Original research articles, review articles, case studies, or something else?
candidate_questions.rationale: This indirectly reveals whether the user is a researcher (original) or a general reader (reviews) and may indicate the need for submission features.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 readers may prefer reviews.
H2 authors typically produce original research.
H3 reviewers often review original research.
H4 members may read broadly.

candidate_questions.question: What does 'manage my involvement' mean to you in this context?
candidate_questions.rationale: Directly asking for the user's interpretation helps distinguish between personal reading management, submission tracking, reviewing tasks, or membership management.

possible_answers.answer: Tracking my submitted articles and reviews
Assignments and decisions as a reviewer/editor

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on reading, so personal management is most likely.

candidate_questions.question: Do you foresee needing to submit your own research articles to the journal?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifying whether the user expects to act as an author directly differentiates H2 (author-focused) from H1, H3, H4.

possible_answers.answer: Yes, I plan to submit articles
No, I only want to read and maybe review

likelihoods.rationale: Readers typically do not submit.
Authors need to submit.
Reviewers may or may not submit.
Society members may contribute but not necessarily submit.

candidate_questions.question: What types of access controls or subscription models should the system support?
candidate_questions.rationale: This question helps separate open-access reader platforms from those that require subscription or institutional access, which aligns with different user roles.

possible_answers.answer: Mix of open access and subscription

likelihoods.rationale: Readers may prefer free or personal subscription.
Author systems often rely on institutional access.
Reviewers may also use institutional access.
Society members might have a mix.

candidate_questions.question: How important is it to have alerts or notifications when new articles in your areas of interest are published?
candidate_questions.rationale: This question probes user preferences for personalized updates, which is more relevant to a reader-focused system but less so for an author/reviewer system where the focus is on manuscript status.

possible_answers.answer: Very important, I want to stay current

likelihoods.rationale: Readers highly value alerts for new content.
Authors may care about status updates more than new articles.
Reviewers may not prioritize article alerts.
Society members may want alerts as part of engagement.

candidate_questions.question: What specific aspects of managing your involvement are most important to you?
candidate_questions.rationale: Directly addresses the core ambiguity of 'manage my involvement' – whether it's personal library management, submission tracking, or membership administration.

possible_answers.answer: Personal reading management (saving articles, history, alerts)

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on reader management, so likely answer A1.
H2 emphasizes submission, so A2 is more probable.
H3 is reviewer/editor oriented, so A3.

candidate_questions.question: Do you anticipate needing to access articles for research purposes, such as data extraction or citation management?
candidate_questions.rationale: Helps distinguish between casual reading and active research use, potentially indicating a society member or researcher role.

possible_answers.answer: No, just reading and basic saving

likelihoods.rationale: H1 reader may not need advanced research tools.
Authors might need citation management for references.
Reviewers may need to analyze data but not necessarily.
Society members could include researchers.

candidate_questions.question: Should the platform support institutional access, such as linking with a university library subscription?
candidate_questions.rationale: Institutional access is common for reader-focused systems, less so for author/reviewer systems, and may indicate a society platform.

likelihoods.rationale: Reader platforms often have institutional access.
Author submission may be individual.
Reviewer systems are typically login-based.
Society members might use institutional or individual.

candidate_questions.rationale: Compliance features are more relevant for professional reviewers or society members than casual readers.

possible_answers.answer: Yes, CME tracking or similar reporting needed

likelihoods.rationale: Casual readers unlikely need compliance.
Authors might need some, but not typical.
Reviewers may need to document activities.
Society members may require CME tracking.

candidate_questions.question: What kind of article search and filtering capabilities do you envision for the platform?
candidate_questions.rationale: This question distinguishes between a simple browse experience (H1) and more advanced search needed for research (H2-H4).

possible_answers.answer: Advanced search with filters (date, category, keywords)

likelihoods.rationale: Reader-focused may be satisfied with basic search.
Authors need to find articles by topic; advanced search likely.
Reviewers may need to search by subject area.
Members may want both basic and advanced options.

candidate_questions.question: Should the system allow you to save articles into personal reading lists or collections?
candidate_questions.rationale: This clarifies the depth of personal management beyond profile: saving articles is key for regular readers (H1, H4) but less so for transactional roles (H2, H3).

likelihoods.rationale: Readers typically want to save articles.
Authors may focus on submission status rather than reading lists.
Reviewers might save articles for review, but not essential.
Members are likely to curate reading lists.

candidate_questions.question: Do you expect the journal's articles to be freely accessible, or should the system manage subscriptions or pay-per-article access?
candidate_questions.rationale: This reveals whether the platform is a public repository (likely H1) or a subscription-based journal (H2-H4).

possible_answers.answer: Managed access with subscriptions or payments

likelihoods.rationale: Reader platforms often prefer open access.
Journals with submissions often have subscription models.
Peer review systems typically live behind paywalls.
Society journals may have mixed models.

candidate_questions.question: Beyond profile management, what additional personal account features would be valuable to you?
candidate_questions.rationale: This open-ended question probes for specific 'manage my involvement' needs beyond profile, such as subscription management, notification preferences, or sharing tools, which differentiate the hypotheses.

likelihoods.rationale: Readers may want alerts for new articles.
Authors mainly need submission tracking beyond profile.
Reviewers might need notification of assignments.
Members may want subscriptions and some sharing.

I prefer the interface elements to use a midnight blue color.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: Basic credit repair website with informational service pages, a self-assessment credit score tool (user inputs data manually), and a contact form that emails leads. No user accounts or CRM integration.
Advanced credit repair website with third-party credit score API integration, user registration to save progress and retrieve scores, and automated lead capture into a CRM for follow-up.
Educational credit repair blog/resource site with a free soft-pull credit score check, an appointment booking system for consultations, and email-based lead notifications.
Compliance-heavy credit repair site with legal disclaimers, a simple lead form that triggers a phone call, and emphasis on trust signals. Credit score check is optional or not included.

Lead capture mechanism (simple email vs CRM vs booking vs phone call)
User account requirement (none vs optional vs mandatory)
update_notes: Initial hypotheses generated based on common variations in credit repair lead generation websites.

candidate_questions.question: How many customer service agents do you anticipate will use the system and what is the expected number of customer records?
candidate_questions.rationale: This directly impacts whether a simple or enterprise solution is appropriate and helps distinguish H1 (small scale) from H2/H4 (larger scale).

possible_answers.answer: Small scale (fewer than 10 agents, hundreds of records)
Medium scale (10-50 agents, thousands of records)
Large scale (more than 50 agents, tens of thousands or more)

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is designed for small operations.
H2 targets large enterprises.
H3 could fit medium to large.
H4 is flexible but often used in larger settings.

candidate_questions.question: What additional information, beyond the client identity, would you like to capture for each call?
candidate_questions.rationale: This clarifies the depth of call logging, distinguishing simple notes (H1) from detailed records (H2).

possible_answers.answer: Only basic notes or issue description
Structured fields like call duration, issue category, and resolution
Full transcripts or recordings and automated metadata

likelihoods.rationale: H1 keeps logging simple.
H2 demands rich call details.
H3 uses structured ticketing fields.
H4 allows customization but often includes structured data.

candidate_questions.question: Where should the system be hosted: on-premise, in the cloud, or a hybrid?
candidate_questions.rationale: Deployment environment can indicate scale and complexity; cloud is common for small systems, on-premise for enterprises.

likelihoods.rationale: Simple systems are typically cloud-based.
Enterprises often prefer on-premise.
Ticketing systems are often cloud-based.
Customizable systems can be either.

candidate_questions.question: What kinds of reports or analytics would you find most valuable?
candidate_questions.rationale: This probes the depth of reporting needed, which could differentiate simpler summaries (H1) from advanced analytics (H2).

possible_answers.answer: Basic summaries: total calls per agent, average duration
Detailed reports: trends over time, issue category breakdowns
Advanced analytics: predictive metrics, dashboards, custom charts

likelihoods.rationale: H1 only needs basic reports.
H2 requires advanced analytics.
H3 includes dashboards and moderate analytics.
H4 can be configured for any level.

conversations.initial_requirements: I need a real estate website where I can browse and search properties.

I prefer the website to use an alice blue background.
I prefer all website elements to be styled using steel blue.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A real estate website focusing on residential properties with basic search by location and price range. Users can view property details and contact agents via phone/email. No user accounts.

A nationwide real estate platform with map-based search, neighborhood statistics, and school information. Allows browsing properties by city/state. No user accounts.

A full-featured real estate website with user accounts, saved searches, property alerts, and direct agent messaging. Includes both residential and commercial listings with advanced filters and map integration.

search sophistication (basic vs advanced filters vs map-based)
user features (no accounts vs accounts with saved searches and alerts)

conversations.initial_requirements: I want an email platform that allows me to write and send emails, and manage my email-related information in one place.

implicit_requirements_for_audit.RequirementText: I prefer to explicitly select recipients before sending emails
I prefer to use predefined email templates to assist with email composition instead of always writing emails from scratch.
I prefer to upload and include attachments directly during the email composition process.
I expect the platform to keep records of sent emails so that I can review and organize past communications.
I expect the platform to maintain structured information about recipients as part of my email management workflow.
I prefer the platform interface to use a cornsilk-colored background.
I prefer the platform’s interface components to use a peru color.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A personal email client for individuals focused on basic compose/send and simple contact management, possibly with a clean interface and minimal extra features.
A business email platform with advanced features like templates, scheduling, multi-account support, and integration with calendars and CRM systems.
A lightweight web-based tool for composing and sending emails, with a focus on email organization (folders, tags, search) and attachments, but no heavy integrations.
A mobile-first email application that syncs across devices, emphasizes quick replies, and includes smart features like smart compose, spam filtering, and email analytics.

conversations.task_name: Driver Recruitment and Job Posting System
conversations.initial_requirements: I want a recruitment website that connects drivers with job opportunities and helps employers find suitable drivers.

implicit_requirements_for_audit.RequirementText: I prefer to use the recruitment website by registering and creating a personal profile before accessing job-related services.
I prefer to apply for driver job opportunities directly through the website.
I prefer to manage and update my contact information myself to ensure accurate communication.
I prefer employers to interact with the system by posting and managing job listings online.
I prefer to communicate with the website operators through a dedicated contact form.
I prefer to navigate the website through clearly separated pages such as home, jobs, drivers, clients, and contact pages.
I expect the website to display available driver job listings in a dedicated jobs section.
I expect the website to present information about the organization through an about us page.
I expect the website to include contact-related information and communication channels on a contact page.
I prefer the website to use a white background.
I prefer the website’s content blocks to use a navy color.

conversation.interviewer: What are the most important features you envision for the recruitment process?
conversation.user: I do not have a strong preference about that, or it is not important to me.

candidate_questions.question: How should the platform generate revenue?
candidate_questions.rationale: The business model is a key differentiator: H1 and H3 typically rely on employer-paid postings, H2 may use a mix, and H4 often involves commission per hire.

likelihoods.rationale: Simple job boards often charge employers per posting.
Verification and matching could be monetized via both posting fees and commissions.
Real-time alerts might be offered free with premium postings.
Managed marketplace typically takes a commission on transactions.

candidate_questions.question: How important is it to verify driver credentials (license, background) before allowing them to apply?
candidate_questions.rationale: This directly tests the trust and safety dimension: H2 and H4 emphasize verification, while H1 and H3 may not require it.

likelihoods.rationale: Simple boards rarely require verification.
Verification is a core feature.
Alerts can work without verification, but may be nice to have.
Trust is essential in a managed marketplace.

candidate_questions.question: How important are real-time notifications for drivers when new jobs are posted?
candidate_questions.rationale: This directly tests the emphasis on real-time features: H3 prioritizes notifications, while others may not.

likelihoods.rationale: Static listings do not require real-time updates.
Matching can benefit from notifications but not essential.
Real-time alerts are the core differentiator.
Marketplace may have notifications but not the primary focus.

likelihoods.rationale: Simple board only needs posting.

Alerts focus on drivers; employer tools minimal.
Managed marketplace requires contract and payment tools.

conversations.task_name: Medical Journal Publishing and Access System
conversations.initial_requirements: I want a website where I can explore and read medical research articles, and manage my involvement with the journal.

implicit_requirements_for_audit.RequirementText: I prefer to locate articles using a built-in search function
I prefer to read articles by opening their full content directly on the website.
I prefer to download articles as PDF files so that I can access them offline.
I prefer to manage my involvement with the journal through a personal account where I can update my profile information.
I prefer the website to use a responsive design that adapts well to different devices and screen sizes.
I prefer the website to use a seashell background color.
I prefer the website components to use a crimson color scheme.

conversation.interviewer: Are there specific features for collaboration or discussion among users?
conversation.user: I do not have a strong preference about that, or it is not important to me.

judge.reasoning: The question asks about collaboration or discussion features, which is not among the remaining hidden implicit requirements.
judge.response: I do not have a strong preference about that, or it is not important to me.

evaluator.reasoning: The question asks about collaboration or discussion features, which is not among the remaining hidden implicit requirements.
evaluator.response: I do not have a strong preference about that, or it is not important to me.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A platform for readers and researchers to access medical articles, with personal account features for saving, preferences, and subscription management.
A full journal management system allowing authors to submit articles, track reviews, and manage submissions, in addition to reading.
A system for reviewers and editors to manage peer review, assign reviewers, make decisions, and communicate, alongside reading access.
A hybrid platform for a medical journal society where members can read, contribute content, participate in discussions, and manage their membership.

hypothesis_disagreements: intended user roles (readers vs authors vs reviewers vs society members)
nature of involvement (personal reading management vs submissions/reviews vs membership)
lifecycle scope (dissemination only vs full submission-to-publication)
collaboration features (none vs limited vs extensive)
update_notes: Initial hypotheses based on initial requirement.

latent_complete_requirement_hypotheses.complete_requirement: A platform for readers and researchers to access medical articles, with personal account features for saving, preferences, and subscription management.
A full journal management system allowing authors to submit articles, track reviews, and manage submissions, in addition to reading.

candidate_questions.question: What types of users do you envision interacting with this system?
candidate_questions.rationale: Identifying primary user roles (readers, authors, reviewers, editors, society members) will help focus the system design.

possible_answers.answer: All roles including editors and society members

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on readers, so readers-only is most likely.
H2 emphasizes authors and reviewers, so that answer type is most probable.
H3 is similar to H2 but for reviewers/editors, still authors/reviewers is most likely.
H4 includes all roles for a society, so all roles is most probable.

candidate_questions.question: What kind of involvement with the journal do you want users to manage?
candidate_questions.rationale: Understanding the nature of involvement (reading management, submissions/reviews, membership) will clarify core functionality.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 emphasizes personal reading management.
H2 focuses on submissions and reviews.
H3 also centers on submissions/reviews (review management).
H4 highlights membership and subscriptions.

candidate_questions.question: Should the system support the entire article lifecycle from submission to publication, or focus mainly on dissemination?
candidate_questions.rationale: This distinguishes between a full publishing platform and a repository or reading site.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is about reading, so dissemination focus.
H2 requires full lifecycle for submissions.
H3 needs full lifecycle for review management.
H4 likely involves both reading and contributions.

candidate_questions.question: Are there specific features for collaboration or discussion among users?
candidate_questions.rationale: Collaboration features (comments, forums, peer review discussions) help differentiate between simple reading and community-oriented platforms.

possible_answers.answer: Extensive collaboration (discussions, forums, peer review communication)

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on individual reading, so minimal collaboration.
H2 may have some collaboration via reviews, but not extensive.
H3 involves extensive peer review communication.
H4 likely includes discussions and community features.

candidate_questions.question: Which users do you envision using this platform?
candidate_questions.rationale: To distinguish between platforms for readers only, authors, reviewers, or all roles.

possible_answers.answer: All including editors and society members

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on readers, so likely 'only readers'.
H2 includes authors, so 'authors and reviewers' likely.
H3 includes reviewers/editors, so might lean to 'all'.
H4 is society-oriented, so 'all including society members'.

candidate_questions.question: What kind of content management do you need? For example, do you need to submit articles or just read?
candidate_questions.rationale: To distinguish H1 (reading only) from H2/H3 (submission and review).

likelihoods.rationale: H1 lacks submission features.
H2 includes submission.
H3 may include submission for reviewers, but not necessarily.
H4 includes content contribution, so submission likely.

candidate_questions.question: Is there a need for peer review functionality?
candidate_questions.rationale: To specifically identify H3 (reviewer/editor system).

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is reader-focused, no review.
H2 for authors may include some review but not central.
H3 centers on review.
H4 may or may not include peer review.

candidate_questions.question: Do you need membership management features like subscriptions or society administration?
candidate_questions.rationale: To identify H4 (society/membership focus).

likelihoods.rationale: H1 may have subscriptions but not full admin.
H2 could include institution subscriptions.
H3 might need editor assignment but not membership.
H4 explicitly involves society membership.

candidate_questions.question: Could you describe what you mean by managing your involvement with the journal?
candidate_questions.rationale: This directly targets the core ambiguity about the user's intended activities beyond reading.

possible_answers.answer: Personal reading management (e.g., save articles, set preferences, subscriptions)
Article submission and tracking (e.g., submit manuscripts, see reviews)
Peer review management (e.g., handle review assignments, write reviews)
Membership management (e.g., pay dues, access member-only content)

likelihoods.rationale: H1 assumes personal reading management.
H2 emphasizes author submission features.
H3 centers on peer review management.
H4 includes membership management as core.

candidate_questions.question: What is your primary role in relation to medical journals?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifying the user's role helps determine which features are essential.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 assumes reader role.
H2 assumes author role.
H3 assumes reviewer/editor role.
H4 allows various roles, but member identity is key.

candidate_questions.question: Do you need the system to support multiple roles (e.g., both reading and submitting), or is a single role sufficient?
candidate_questions.rationale: This determines whether the system must handle multiple user types and workflows.

possible_answers.answer: Multiple roles should be supported for the same user
The system should support different roles for different users

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on reader role but may accept multiple.
H2 involves authors who may also read.
H3 involves reviewers who are often also authors.
H4 typically has multiple distinct roles (members, editors).

candidate_questions.question: What kind of content are you most interested in? Original research articles, review articles, case studies, or something else?
candidate_questions.rationale: This indirectly reveals whether the user is a researcher (original) or a general reader (reviews) and may indicate the need for submission features.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 readers may prefer reviews.
H2 authors typically produce original research.
H3 reviewers often review original research.
H4 members may read broadly.

Remaining uncertainty: depth of search, saving capabilities, and access control.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A reader-focused platform with personal accounts for profile management, article browsing, and optional saving/reading lists. No submission or collaboration features.
A platform for authors to submit and track manuscripts, in addition to reading. Includes personal account for profile and submission status.
A platform for reviewers/editors to manage peer review and decision-making, plus reading access. Personal account for profile and review assignments.
A society-driven platform where members read articles, manage their membership, and contribute content or discussions. Personal account includes profile and membership settings.

hypothesis_disagreements: intended user roles (readers vs authors vs reviewers vs society members)
nature of 'manage my involvement' (personal reading management only vs submission/review vs membership)
asked_questions: Are there specific features for collaboration or discussion among users?
Which users do you envision using this platform?
What is your primary role in relation to medical journals?
Do you foresee needing to submit your own research articles to the journal?
What specific aspects of managing your involvement are most important to you?
Should the system allow you to save articles into personal reading lists or collections?
update_notes: User confirmed personal account for profile management, reinforcing a reader or member view.
User neutral on saving articles, so that feature is not critical.
Probabilities adjusted: H1 increased, H2 and H3 decreased slightly, H4 unchanged.

candidate_questions.question: Who is the primary audience for this medical journal platform?
candidate_questions.rationale: Distinguishes between general readers, authors, reviewers, and society members, which directly impacts feature priorities.

possible_answers.answer: General public or healthcare professionals interested in reading articles
Authors who submit their own research manuscripts
Reviewers or editors involved in peer review
Members of a specific medical society or association

likelihoods.rationale: Reader-focused hypothesis expects general audience.
Author hypothesis expects primarily authors.
Reviewer hypothesis expects reviewers/editors.
Society hypothesis expects members.

candidate_questions.question: How should users access the articles on the platform?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies whether the content is open, subscription, or membership-only, which is a key design dimension.

likelihoods.rationale: Reader platform could be open or subscription.
Author platform may have limited access via submission.
Reviewer platform may have mixed access.
Society platform likely has member-only access.

candidate_questions.question: What kind of browsing and search functionality would be important for you?
candidate_questions.rationale: Differentiates between simple and advanced search needs, which affects system complexity.

possible_answers.answer: Simple search by title or author
Advanced search with multiple filters (e.g., date, topic, journal)

likelihoods.rationale: Readers might want advanced filtering.
Authors may need advanced search for references.
Reviewers need precise search.
Society members may have varied needs.

candidate_questions.question: Should the platform cover multiple journals or is it dedicated to one journal?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies scope: single journal vs multi-journal, which affects hypothesis alignment.

possible_answers.answer: Multiple journals from different publishers or fields
Multiple journals from a single publisher or society

likelihoods.rationale: Reader platform often aggregates multiple journals.
Author platform is often per journal.
Reviewer platform per journal.
Society platform likely covers its own journals.

bed_decision_trace.eig_formula: EIG(q)=H[p(h)] - sum_a p(a|q) H[p(h|a,q)], with rolling hypotheses from the evolving belief state.
